docs.title: Species Explorer
docs.text: QGIS Plugin for Exploring Biodiversity Data from GBIF

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Species Explorer is a QGIS plugin that allows you to quickly fetch and visualize species occurrence data from the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF).

With Species Explorer, you can:

  • Search for species by scientific or common name
  • Fetch occurrence records from GBIF's extensive database
  • Visualize species distributions directly in QGIS
  • Analyze biodiversity patterns with powerful GIS tools

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
1. Install the plugin from the QGIS Plugin Manager
  2. Open Species Explorer from the Plugins menu
  3. Search for a species by name
  4. Click Fetch to download occurrence data
  5. Explore the data in QGIS!

Get Started

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Species Search

    Search for species using scientific names, common names, or taxonomic hierarchy.

  • GBIF Integration

    Direct integration with GBIF's REST API to fetch occurrence records.

  • Point Layer Creation

    Automatically creates point layers with occurrence locations and attributes.

  • Styled Visualization

    Includes default styling with clustered point visualization.

docs.title: GBIF
docs.text: This plugin would not be possible without the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF), which provides open access to biodiversity data.

docs.title: pygbif
docs.text: The GBIF API utilities were inspired by pygbif, the Python client for GBIF, created by Scott Chamberlain.

docs.title: QGIS Community
docs.text: Thanks to the entire QGIS community for creating such a powerful and extensible GIS platform.

docs.title: Data License
docs.text:
Data fetched from GBIF is subject to GBIF's data licensing:

  • GBIF-mediated data is open data
  • Individual datasets may have specific licenses (CC0, CC-BY, CC-BY-NC)
  • Always cite data sources appropriately

See GBIF Terms of Use for details.

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: Administrator Guide
docs.text: This guide is for system administrators deploying Species Explorer in enterprise environments.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Species Explorer is a QGIS plugin that requires:

  • QGIS 3.0 or later
  • Network access to GBIF API (api.gbif.org)
  • Python 3.9+ (bundled with QGIS)

docs.title: Option 1: QGIS Plugin Repository (Recommended)
docs.text:
For individual workstations:

  1. Users install via QGIS Plugin Manager
  2. Automatic updates when new versions are released
  3. No administrative intervention required

docs.title: Option 2: Custom Plugin Repository
docs.text:
For enterprise deployments with controlled software:

  1. Download plugin ZIP from GitHub Releases
  2. Host on internal file server or web server
  3. Configure QGIS to use custom repository

Repository XML Format:

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<plugins>
  <pyqgis_plugin name="Species Explorer" version="0.3.0">
    <description>Explore species occurrence data from GBIF</description>
    <about>QGIS plugin for biodiversity data visualization</about>
    <version>0.3.0</version>
    <qgis_minimum_version>3.0</qgis_minimum_version>
    <homepage>https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer</homepage>
    <file_name>SpeciesExplorer-0.3.0.zip</file_name>
    <author_name>Kartoza</author_name>
    <download_url>https://your-server/plugins/SpeciesExplorer-0.3.0.zip</download_url>
  </pyqgis_plugin>
</plugins>

QGIS Configuration:

  1. Go to Settings → Options → General → Plugin Repositories
  2. Add your custom repository URL
  3. Users can install from Plugin Manager

docs.title: Option 3: Pre-installed QGIS Profile
docs.text:
For standardized deployments:

  1. Create QGIS profile with pre-installed plugins
  2. Deploy profile to users via:
  3. Roaming profiles (Windows)
  4. Configuration management (Ansible, Puppet)
  5. Disk imaging

Profile Location:

Platform Path Linux ~/.local/share/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/ Windows %APPDATA%\QGIS\QGIS3\profiles\ macOS ~/Library/Application Support/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/

docs.title: Firewall Rules
docs.text:
Allow outbound HTTPS (443) to:

Host Purpose api.gbif.org GBIF REST API www.gbif.org GBIF website (optional)

docs.title: Proxy Configuration
docs.text:
Species Explorer uses QGIS native networking, which respects system/QGIS proxy settings.

Configure in QGIS:

  1. Settings → Options → Network
  2. Set proxy type and credentials
  3. Species Explorer will use these settings

Environment Variables:

export HTTP_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
export HTTPS_PROXY=http://proxy.example.com:8080
export NO_PROXY=localhost,127.0.0.1

docs.title: SSL/TLS
docs.text:
• GBIF API uses TLS 1.2+
  • Ensure CA certificates are up to date
  • For corporate SSL inspection, add certificates to system trust store

docs.title: Network Bandwidth
docs.text:
Operation Typical Size Species search 1-10 KB Taxonomy lookup 1-5 KB Occurrence fetch 100 KB - 50 MB

Note: Large species datasets (millions of records) may download significant data.

docs.title: Memory
docs.text:
• Occurrence data is loaded into RAM
  • Memory usage scales with record count
  • Typical usage: 100 MB per 100,000 records

docs.title: Disk
docs.text:
• Plugin installation: ~2 MB
  • Downloaded data: Stored in memory, saved to project file

docs.title: Data Privacy
docs.text:
• No user data is sent to GBIF
  • Only species names are transmitted
  • Downloaded data may contain location information

docs.title: Network Security
docs.text:
• All connections use HTTPS
  • No authentication required for GBIF API
  • Plugin does not store credentials

docs.title: Plugin Security
docs.text:
• Open source (auditable code)
  • No external dependencies beyond QGIS
  • REUSE compliant licensing

docs.title: Log Files
docs.text:
QGIS logs are available in:

  • View → Log Messages panel
  • ~/.local/share/QGIS/QGIS3/logs/ (Linux)

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text: Issue Solution Connection timeout Check firewall/proxy SSL errors Update CA certificates Slow downloads Normal for large datasets Plugin not loading Check Python errors in log

docs.title: Windows (PowerShell)
docs.text:
$pluginPath = "$env:APPDATA\QGIS\QGIS3\profiles\default\python\plugins"
$pluginUrl = "https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer/releases/latest/download/SpeciesExplorer.zip"

# Download and extract
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $pluginUrl -OutFile "$env:TEMP\SpeciesExplorer.zip"
Expand-Archive -Path "$env:TEMP\SpeciesExplorer.zip" -DestinationPath $pluginPath -Force

docs.title: Linux (Bash)
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
PLUGIN_PATH="$HOME/.local/share/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/default/python/plugins"
PLUGIN_URL="https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer/releases/latest/download/SpeciesExplorer.zip"

mkdir -p "$PLUGIN_PATH"
curl -L "$PLUGIN_URL" -o /tmp/SpeciesExplorer.zip
unzip -o /tmp/SpeciesExplorer.zip -d "$PLUGIN_PATH"

docs.title: Ansible Playbook
docs.text:
---
- name: Install Species Explorer plugin
  hosts: workstations
  tasks:
    - name: Download plugin
      get_url:
        url: https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer/releases/latest/download/SpeciesExplorer.zip
        dest: /tmp/SpeciesExplorer.zip

    - name: Extract plugin
      unarchive:
        src: /tmp/SpeciesExplorer.zip
        dest: "{{ ansible_env.HOME }}/.local/share/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/default/python/plugins/"
        remote_src: yes

docs.title: Plugin Won't Load
docs.text:
1. Check QGIS version (3.0+ required)
  2. Check Python version (3.9+ recommended)
  3. Review QGIS log for errors
  4. Verify plugin is enabled in Plugin Manager

docs.title: Network Issues
docs.text:
1. Test connectivity: curl https://api.gbif.org/v1/species/1
  2. Check proxy settings in QGIS
  3. Verify firewall rules
  4. Test SSL: openssl s_client -connect api.gbif.org:443

docs.title: Performance Issues
docs.text:
1. Large datasets are expected to be slow
  2. Consider filtering data after import
  3. Use SSD for QGIS projects
  4. Allocate sufficient RAM

docs.title: Developer Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Species Explorer Developer Guide! This section covers development setup, architecture, and contribution guidelines.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
• Setup

    Set up your development environment

  • Architecture

    Understand the plugin structure

  • Contributing

    How to contribute to the project

  • API Reference

    Technical documentation

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer.git
cd SpeciesExplorer

docs.title: 2. Enter Development Environment
docs.text:
Using Nix (recommended): 

nix develop

This provides all development tools including:

  • Python with QGIS bindings
  • Testing tools (pytest, coverage)
  • Code quality tools (black, flake8, isort)
  • Documentation tools (mkdocs)

docs.title: 3. Symlink to QGIS
docs.text: nix run .#symlink

docs.title: 4. Launch QGIS
docs.text: nix run .#qgis

docs.title: Development Workflow
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Code] --> B[Format]
    B --> C[Lint]
    C --> D[Test]
    D --> E[Commit]
    E --> F[PR]

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text: Command Description nix run .#format Format code with black and isort nix run .#lint Run linters (flake8, pylint) nix run .#test Run test suite with coverage nix run .#docs-serve Serve documentation locally nix run .#checks Run pre-commit checks

docs.title: Code Standards
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+ target version
  • 120 character line length
  • Black for code formatting
  • Google-style docstrings
  • Type hints encouraged

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
Run the test suite:

nix run .#test

View coverage report:

open htmlcov/index.html

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
Build and serve docs locally:

nix run .#docs-serve

Open http://localhost:8000 in your browser.

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
• Open an issue
  • Start a discussion
  • Contact Kartoza

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text: Technical documentation for Species Explorer modules and classes.

docs.title: Module Overview
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[species_explorer] --> B[species_explorer_dialog]
    B --> C[gbifutils]
    B --> D[gbif_fetcher]

docs.title: Class: SpeciesExplorer
docs.text:
Main plugin class that handles QGIS integration.

class SpeciesExplorer:
    """QGIS Plugin for exploring species occurrence data from GBIF."""

docs.title: __init__(iface: QgisInterface)
docs.text:
Initialize the plugin.

Parameters:

Name Type Description iface QgisInterface QGIS interface object

Attributes Set:

  • self.iface - QGIS interface reference
  • self.actions - List of plugin actions
  • self.menu - Menu name string
  • self.toolbar - QToolBar instance
  • self.pluginIsActive - Boolean state flag
  • self.dlg - Dialog instance (created on first run)

docs.title: initGui()
docs.text:
Initialize the plugin GUI elements. Creates toolbar action and menu entry.

Effects:

  • Creates toolbar with plugin icon
  • Adds menu entry under Plugins

docs.title: run()
docs.text:
Show the Species Explorer dialog.

Behavior:

  • Creates dialog on first call
  • Shows dialog as non-modal window

docs.title: unload()
docs.text:
Clean up plugin resources when unloading.

Effects:

  • Removes toolbar actions
  • Removes menu entries
  • Deletes toolbar

docs.title: add_action(icon_path, text, callback, ...)
docs.text:
Helper method to add toolbar/menu actions.

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description icon_path str Required Path to icon file text str Required Action text callback callable Required Function to call enabled_flag bool True Initial enabled state add_to_menu bool True Add to menu add_to_toolbar bool True Add to toolbar status_tip str None Status bar tip whats_this str None What's This help parent QWidget None Parent widget

Returns:

QAction - The created action

docs.title: tr(message: str) -> str
docs.text:
Translation helper for internationalization.

Parameters:

  • message - String to translate

Returns:

Translated string

docs.title: Class: SpeciesExplorerDialog
docs.text:
Main dialog class with UI logic.

class SpeciesExplorerDialog(QDialog):
    """Dialog for Species Explorer plugin."""

docs.title: __init__(parent: QWidget = None)
docs.text:
Initialize the dialog.

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description parent QWidget None Parent widget

Initialization:

  • Loads UI from .ui file
  • Sets up signal/slot connections
  • Initializes Kartoza branding components
  • Creates status label

docs.title: Properties
docs.text: Property Type Description search_text QLineEdit Species name input search_button QPushButton Find button results_list QListWidget Search results taxonomy_list QListWidget Taxonomy display fetch_button QPushButton Fetch button status_label StatusLabel Status display

docs.title: find()
docs.text:
Search GBIF for species matching the search text.

Behavior:

  1. Gets search text from input field
  2. Calls name_parser() to parse scientific name
  3. Queries GBIF /species/search endpoint
  4. Filters results to ACCEPTED species only
  5. Populates results list with unique canonical names

Side Effects:

  • Updates results_list widget
  • Updates status label
  • Stores species keys in item data

docs.title: select(item: QListWidgetItem)
docs.text:
Handle species selection from results list.

Parameters:

  • item - Selected list item

Behavior:

  1. Gets GBIF taxon key from item data
  2. Queries GBIF /species/{key} endpoint
  3. Extracts taxonomic hierarchy
  4. Displays in taxonomy list

Displayed Fields:

  • Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species
  • Taxon ID, Canonical Name, Accepted Name

docs.title: fetch()
docs.text:
Start async background task to fetch occurrence data.

Behavior:

  1. Gets species name from selected item
  2. Creates GBIFFetchTask instance
  3. Connects completion callback
  4. Adds task to QGIS task manager
  5. Updates UI to fetching state

Side Effects:

  • Disables fetch button during operation
  • Updates status with progress
  • Adds layer to project on success

docs.title: _on_fetch_finished(task: GBIFFetchTask)
docs.text:
Handle completion of async fetch task.

Parameters:

  • task - Completed fetch task

Behavior:

  • If successful: adds layer to project, shows success status
  • If failed: shows error message in red

docs.title: _set_status(message: str, is_error: bool = False)
docs.text:
Update status message display.

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description message str Required Status message is_error bool False Show as error (red)

docs.title: _set_fetching_state(is_fetching: bool)
docs.text:
Enable/disable UI controls during fetch.

Parameters:

  • is_fetching - True to disable controls

Effects:

  • Disables/enables fetch button
  • Disables/enables search controls
  • Updates cursor

docs.title: closeEvent(event: QCloseEvent)
docs.text:
Handle dialog close, cancelling any running tasks.

Behavior:

  • Cancels active fetch task if running
  • Accepts close event

docs.title: Class: GBIFFetchTask
docs.text:
Async background task for fetching GBIF occurrence data.

class GBIFFetchTask(QgsTask):
    """Non-blocking task for fetching species occurrences from GBIF."""

docs.title: __init__(species_name: str)
docs.text:
Create a new fetch task.

Parameters:

Name Type Description species_name str Scientific name to search

Attributes:

Attribute Type Description species_name str Search query layer QgsVectorLayer Result layer (set on success) error_message str Error message (set on failure) records list Fetched occurrence records

docs.title: Constants
docs.text:
GBIF_PAGE_SIZE = 300      # Records per API request
GBIF_MAX_OFFSET = 100000  # GBIF's hard limit

docs.title: run() -> bool
docs.text:
Main task execution (runs in background thread).

Returns:

True on success, False on failure

Behavior:

  1. Fetches occurrences with pagination
  2. Processes records
  3. Creates vector layer
  4. Returns success status

docs.title: _fetch_occurrences()
docs.text:
Orchestrate paginated record fetching.

Behavior:

  • Makes requests until all records fetched or limit reached
  • Reports progress during fetch
  • Handles pagination automatically

docs.title: _make_request(offset: int) -> dict
docs.text:
Make single HTTP request to GBIF.

Parameters:

  • offset - Pagination offset

Returns:

JSON response as dictionary

Raises:

  • GBIFNetworkError on connection failure

docs.title: _process_records(response: dict)
docs.text:
Process occurrence records from API response.

Parameters:

  • response - GBIF API response

Behavior:

  • Filters records with valid coordinates
  • Sanitizes field names
  • Stores in self.records

docs.title: _create_layer()
docs.text:
Create QgsVectorLayer from fetched records.

Behavior:

  1. Determines field schema from records
  2. Creates memory layer
  3. Adds features with geometries
  4. Sets layer name to species name

Result:

Sets self.layer to created layer

docs.title: finished(result: bool)
docs.text:
Called when task completes (in main thread).

Parameters:

  • result - Success status from run()

Note:

This is where callbacks should be connected to handle results.

docs.title: Function: fetch_species_async
docs.text:
Convenience function for async fetching.

def fetch_species_async(
    species_name: str,
    on_finished: Callable[[GBIFFetchTask], None]
) -> GBIFFetchTask

Parameters:

Name Type Description species_name str Species to search on_finished callable Callback function

Returns:

GBIFFetchTask - The created task (already started)

Example:

def handle_result(task):
    if task.layer:
        QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(task.layer)
    else:
        print(f"Error: {task.error_message}")

fetch_species_async("Panthera leo", handle_result)

docs.title: Module: gbifutils
docs.text: Utilities for interacting with the GBIF API.

docs.title: name_parser(name: str) -> dict
docs.text:
Parse a scientific name using GBIF name parser.

Parameters:

  • name - Scientific name to parse

Returns:

Dictionary with parsed name components:

{
    'scientificName': 'Panthera leo',
    'canonicalName': 'Panthera leo',
    'canonicalNameComplete': 'Panthera leo',
    'genus': 'Panthera',
    'specificEpithet': 'leo',
    'type': 'SCIENTIFIC',
    'parsed': True
}

Raises:

  • NoResultException if parsing fails

docs.title: name_usage(key: int, ...) -> dict
docs.text:
Get species information by GBIF taxon key.

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description key int None GBIF taxon key name str None Alternative: search by name data str 'all' Data subset to return language str None Preferred language rank str None Taxonomic rank filter

Returns:

Dictionary with species details:

{
    'key': 5219404,
    'scientificName': 'Panthera leo (Linnaeus, 1758)',
    'canonicalName': 'Panthera leo',
    'kingdom': 'Animalia',
    'phylum': 'Chordata',
    'class': 'Mammalia',
    'order': 'Carnivora',
    'family': 'Felidae',
    'genus': 'Panthera',
    'species': 'Panthera leo',
    'kingdomKey': 1,
    'phylumKey': 44,
    'classKey': 359,
    'orderKey': 732,
    'familyKey': 9703,
    'genusKey': 2435194,
    'speciesKey': 5219404,
    'taxonomicStatus': 'ACCEPTED',
    'rank': 'SPECIES'
}

docs.title: gbif_GET(url: str, args: dict = None) -> dict
docs.text:
Make a GET request to the GBIF API.

Parameters:

Name Type Default Description url str Required Full API URL args dict None Query parameters

Returns:

JSON response as dictionary

Raises:

  • GBIFNetworkError on connection failure
  • NoResultException on empty response

Implementation:

  • Uses QgsFileDownloader for network requests
  • Respects QGIS proxy settings
  • Handles SSL certificates properly

docs.title: NoResultException
docs.text:
Raised when GBIF returns no results.

class NoResultException(Exception):
    """Raised when GBIF API returns no results."""

docs.title: GBIFNetworkError
docs.text:
Raised for network/connection failures.

class GBIFNetworkError(Exception):
    """Raised when network request fails."""

docs.text: Load stylesheet from resources.

docs.text: Get path to resources directory.

docs.title: GBIF API
docs.text: GBIF_BASE_URL = "https://api.gbif.org/v1"

docs.title: Coordinate Reference System
docs.text: DEFAULT_CRS = "EPSG:4326"  # WGS 84

docs.title: Pagination
docs.text:
GBIF_PAGE_SIZE = 300
GBIF_MAX_OFFSET = 100000

docs.title: Task Completion Pattern
docs.text:
The async fetcher uses a callback pattern for completion:

def on_task_finished(task: GBIFFetchTask):
    """Handle task completion."""
    if task.layer:
        # Success - layer is ready
        QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(task.layer)
    else:
        # Failure - check error
        print(f"Error: {task.error_message}")

# Connect callback
task = GBIFFetchTask("Panthera leo")
task.taskCompleted.connect(lambda: on_task_finished(task))
QgsApplication.taskManager().addTask(task)

docs.title: Basic Species Search
docs.text:
from species_explorer.gbifutils import name_parser, name_usage

# Parse a species name
parsed = name_parser("Panthera leo")
print(f"Canonical: {parsed['canonicalName']}")

# Get species details by key
species = name_usage(5219404)
print(f"Kingdom: {species['kingdom']}")
print(f"Family: {species['family']}")

docs.title: Fetch Occurrences Async
docs.text:
from species_explorer.gbif_fetcher import fetch_species_async
from qgis.core import QgsProject

def handle_result(task):
    if task.layer:
        QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(task.layer)
        print(f"Added {task.layer.featureCount()} features")
    else:
        print(f"Error: {task.error_message}")

fetch_species_async("Panthera leo", handle_result)

docs.title: Create Layer Manually
docs.text:
from qgis.core import (
    QgsVectorLayer, QgsFeature, QgsGeometry,
    QgsPointXY, QgsField, QgsFields
)
from PyQt5.QtCore import QVariant

# Create memory layer
layer = QgsVectorLayer(
    "Point?crs=EPSG:4326",
    "Occurrences",
    "memory"
)

# Define fields
fields = QgsFields()
fields.append(QgsField('gbifID', QVariant.String))
fields.append(QgsField('scientificName', QVariant.String))
layer.dataProvider().addAttributes(fields)
layer.updateFields()

# Add features
for occ in occurrences:
    feature = QgsFeature()
    feature.setGeometry(QgsGeometry.fromPointXY(
        QgsPointXY(
            float(occ['decimalLongitude']),
            float(occ['decimalLatitude'])
        )
    ))
    feature.setAttributes([
        occ['gbifID'],
        occ['scientificName']
    ])
    layer.dataProvider().addFeature(feature)

layer.updateExtents()

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: This document describes the architecture and design of Species Explorer.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Species Explorer is a QGIS Python plugin that interfaces with the GBIF REST API to fetch and visualize species occurrence data. The plugin uses asynchronous background tasks for non-blocking data fetching.

graph TB
    subgraph QGIS
        A[Plugin Entry<br/>__init__.py] --> B[Main Plugin<br/>species_explorer.py]
        B --> C[Dialog<br/>species_explorer_dialog.py]
        C --> D[GBIF Utils<br/>gbifutils.py]
        C --> E[Async Fetcher<br/>gbif_fetcher.py]
        E --> F[Layer Creation]
        F --> G[QGIS Map Canvas]
    end

    subgraph Branding
        H[Kartoza Header]
        I[Kartoza Footer]
        J[Status Label]
    end

    subgraph External
        K[GBIF API<br/>api.gbif.org/v1]
    end

    C --> H
    C --> I
    C --> J
    D <--> K
    E <--> K

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
SpeciesExplorer/
├── species_explorer/              # Main plugin package (deployed to QGIS)
│   ├── __init__.py               # Plugin initialization & classFactory
│   ├── species_explorer.py       # Main plugin class
│   ├── species_explorer_dialog.py        # Dialog UI logic
│   ├── species_explorer_dialog_base.ui   # Qt Designer UI file
│   ├── gbifutils.py              # GBIF API utilities (sync)
│   ├── gbif_fetcher.py           # Async GBIF fetcher (QgsTask)
│   ├── resources.py              # Compiled Qt resources
│   ├── resources.qrc             # Qt resource definitions
│   ├── icon.png                  # Plugin icon
│   ├── metadata.txt              # QGIS plugin metadata
│   ├── gui/
│   │   └── kartoza_branding.py   # Kartoza branding components
│   └── resources/
│       └── styles/kartoza.qss    # Custom QSS stylesheet
├── test/                          # Test suite
├── docs/                          # MkDocs documentation
├── .github/workflows/             # CI/CD workflows
├── flake.nix                      # Nix development environment
└── mkdocs.yml                     # Documentation configuration

Important: Only the species_explorer/ folder is deployed to QGIS. All other files are for development and documentation.

docs.title: Plugin Entry Point
docs.text:
File: species_explorer/__init__.py

The entry point for QGIS plugin loading:

def classFactory(iface):
    from .species_explorer import SpeciesExplorer
    return SpeciesExplorer(iface)

docs.title: Main Plugin Class
docs.text:
File: species_explorer/species_explorer.py

Handles plugin lifecycle:

Method Purpose __init__(iface) Initialize with QGIS interface initGui() Create toolbar and menu items run() Show the Species Explorer dialog unload() Clean up on plugin unload add_action() Helper for toolbar actions tr() Translation support

docs.title: Dialog Class
docs.text:
File: species_explorer/species_explorer_dialog.py

Main user interface and business logic:

Method Purpose find() Search GBIF for species select(item) Display taxonomic hierarchy fetch() Start async background fetch _on_fetch_finished(task) Handle fetch completion _set_status() Update status display _set_fetching_state() Toggle UI during fetch closeEvent() Cancel tasks on close

UI Elements:

  • search_text - Species name input
  • search_button - Trigger search
  • results_list - Matching species list
  • taxonomy_list - Taxonomic hierarchy display
  • fetch_button - Download occurrences
  • status_label - Progress/error display

docs.title: Async GBIF Fetcher
docs.text:
File: species_explorer/gbif_fetcher.py

Non-blocking background task for fetching occurrence data:

class GBIFFetchTask(QgsTask):
    """Background task for fetching GBIF occurrences."""

Key Features:

  • Extends QgsTask for QGIS task management
  • Runs in background thread without blocking UI
  • Handles paginated GBIF API responses
  • Progress reporting capability
  • User-cancellable
  • Creates QgsVectorLayer from fetched data

Configuration Constants:

GBIF_PAGE_SIZE = 300      # Records per request
GBIF_MAX_OFFSET = 100000  # GBIF API hard limit

Methods:

Method Purpose run() Main execution in background _fetch_occurrences() Orchestrate pagination _make_request(offset) Single HTTP request _process_records() Parse occurrence data _create_layer() Create QgsVectorLayer finished(result) Callback on completion

Usage:

from species_explorer.gbif_fetcher import fetch_species_async

def on_complete(task):
    if task.layer:
        QgsProject.instance().addMapLayer(task.layer)

fetch_species_async("Panthera leo", on_complete)

docs.title: GBIF Utilities
docs.text:
File: species_explorer/gbifutils.py

Synchronous API interaction layer (adapted from pygbif):

Function Purpose gbif_GET(url, args) HTTP GET with QGIS networking name_parser(name) Parse scientific names name_usage(key, ...) Look up taxon details

Exception Classes:

class NoResultException(Exception):
    """Raised when GBIF returns no results."""

class GBIFNetworkError(Exception):
    """Raised for network failures."""

Networking:

  • Uses QgsFileDownloader for QGIS proxy/SSL support
  • Synchronous wrapper with QEventLoop
  • No external HTTP libraries required

docs.title: Kartoza Branding
docs.text:
File: species_explorer/gui/kartoza_branding.py

Consistent branding components:

Brand Colors:

KARTOZA_GREEN_DARK = "#589632"
KARTOZA_GREEN_LIGHT = "#93b023"
KARTOZA_GOLD = "#E8B849"

Components:

Class Purpose KartozaHeader Branded header with logo KartozaFooter Footer with links StatusLabel Color-coded status display

docs.title: Complete Workflow Sequence
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Dialog
    participant GBIFUtils
    participant GBIFFetchTask
    participant GBIF API
    participant QGIS

    User->>Dialog: Enter species name
    User->>Dialog: Click Find
    Dialog->>GBIFUtils: name_parser(name)
    GBIFUtils->>GBIF API: GET /parser/name
    GBIF API-->>GBIFUtils: Parsed name
    Dialog->>GBIFUtils: species/search
    GBIFUtils->>GBIF API: GET /species/search
    GBIF API-->>GBIFUtils: Matching taxa
    GBIFUtils-->>Dialog: Results list
    Dialog-->>User: Display results

    User->>Dialog: Select species
    Dialog->>GBIFUtils: name_usage(key)
    GBIFUtils->>GBIF API: GET /species/{key}
    GBIF API-->>GBIFUtils: Taxonomy data
    GBIFUtils-->>Dialog: Species details
    Dialog-->>User: Display taxonomy

    User->>Dialog: Click Fetch
    Dialog->>GBIFFetchTask: Start background task
    activate GBIFFetchTask

    loop Paginated fetching
        GBIFFetchTask->>GBIF API: GET /occurrence/search
        GBIF API-->>GBIFFetchTask: Occurrence page
        GBIFFetchTask->>Dialog: Progress update
    end

    GBIFFetchTask->>GBIFFetchTask: Create layer
    GBIFFetchTask-->>Dialog: Task complete
    deactivate GBIFFetchTask

    Dialog->>QGIS: Add layer to project
    QGIS-->>User: Display points

docs.title: GBIF Endpoints Used
docs.text: Endpoint Method Purpose /parser/name GET Parse scientific names /species/search GET Search for species /species/{key} GET Get species details /occurrence/search GET Search occurrences

docs.title: Query Parameters
docs.text:
Species Search:

GET /species/search?q={query}&rank=SPECIES&status=ACCEPTED

Occurrence Search:

GET /occurrence/search?scientificName={name}&limit=300&offset={offset}

docs.title: Response Handling
docs.text:
• JSON responses parsed to Python dicts
  • Records filtered for valid coordinates
  • Field names sanitized for QGIS compatibility

docs.title: Layer Creation
docs.text:
When data is fetched:

  1. Create memory layer with Point geometry (EPSG:4326)
  2. Define attribute fields dynamically from response
  3. Add features for each occurrence with coordinates
  4. Add layer to QGIS project

docs.title: Field Schema
docs.text:
Fields are created dynamically from GBIF response. Common fields:

Field Type Description gbifID String Unique identifier scientificName String Full scientific name decimalLatitude String Latitude decimalLongitude String Longitude eventDate String Observation date basisOfRecord String Record type countryCode String ISO country code institutionCode String Data provider

Field Name Sanitization:

  • Alphanumeric characters and underscores only
  • Maximum 60 characters
  • All stored as String(255) for compatibility

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Errors are handled at multiple levels:

graph TD
    A[Network Error] --> B[GBIFNetworkError]
    C[No Results] --> D[NoResultException]
    E[Invalid Data] --> F[Filtered/Skipped]

    B --> G[Status Label - Red]
    D --> G
    F --> H[Warning in Log]

    G --> I[User Notification]

Error Types:

  1. Network errors - Connection timeouts, HTTP errors
  2. API errors - Invalid responses, rate limiting
  3. Data errors - Missing coordinates, invalid values

Display:

  • StatusLabel shows errors in red
  • Successful operations shown in green
  • QGIS log contains detailed messages

docs.title: Why QgsTask?
docs.text:
• Non-blocking UI - User can interact during downloads
  • Progress reporting - Real-time status updates
  • Cancellation support - User can cancel long operations
  • Thread safety - Proper handling of Qt signals/slots
  • QGIS integration - Task manager visibility

docs.title: Task Lifecycle
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending: Created
    Pending --> Running: Started
    Running --> Running: Progress update
    Running --> Completed: Success
    Running --> Failed: Error
    Running --> Cancelled: User cancel
    Completed --> [*]
    Failed --> [*]
    Cancelled --> [*]

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
The test suite covers:

  • Plugin initialization
  • GBIF API integration
  • Dialog functionality
  • Layer creation
  • Error handling
  • Async task behavior

Run tests with:

nix run .#test

docs.title: Adding New Data Sources
docs.text:
To add additional data sources:

  1. Create new utility module (similar to gbifutils.py)
  2. Create async fetcher (similar to gbif_fetcher.py)
  3. Add UI elements to dialog
  4. Update layer creation logic

docs.title: Custom Styling
docs.text:
Modify the default layer styling by:

  1. Creating a QML style file
  2. Applying style after layer creation
  3. Or letting users apply their own styles

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Species Explorer! This guide explains how to contribute to the project.

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: Please be respectful and constructive in all interactions. We welcome contributors of all backgrounds and experience levels.

docs.title: Report Bugs
docs.text:
Found a bug? Please open an issue with:

  • Clear description of the problem
  • Steps to reproduce
  • Expected vs actual behavior
  • QGIS and plugin versions
  • Screenshots if applicable

docs.title: Suggest Features
docs.text:
Have an idea? Open a feature request with:

  • Clear description of the feature
  • Use case and benefits
  • Possible implementation approach

docs.title: Improve Documentation
docs.text:
Documentation improvements are always welcome:

  • Fix typos and errors
  • Add examples and tutorials
  • Improve explanations
  • Translate documentation

docs.title: Submit Code
docs.text: Ready to code? Follow these steps:

docs.title: 1. Fork and Clone
docs.text:
# Fork on GitHub, then:
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/SpeciesExplorer.git
cd SpeciesExplorer
git remote add upstream https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer.git

docs.title: 2. Create a Branch
docs.text:
git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name
# or
git checkout -b fix/your-bug-fix

Use descriptive branch names:

  • feature/add-export-csv
  • fix/handle-empty-results
  • docs/improve-installation

docs.title: 3. Set Up Environment
docs.text:
nix develop
pre-commit install

docs.title: 4. Make Changes
docs.text:
Write your code following our standards:

  • Format with black: nix run .#format
  • Lint with flake8: nix run .#lint
  • Test your changes: nix run .#test

docs.title: 5. Commit Changes
docs.text:
Write clear commit messages:

git commit -m "Add CSV export functionality

- Add export_to_csv() function
- Add Export button to dialog
- Update documentation"

docs.title: 6. Push and Create PR
docs.text:
git push origin feature/your-feature-name

Then open a Pull Request on GitHub.

docs.title: Python Style
docs.text:
• Python 3.9+ compatibility
  • 120 character line length
  • Black formatting (automatic with pre-commit)
  • isort for imports (automatic with pre-commit)

docs.title: Docstrings
docs.text:
Use Google-style docstrings:

def fetch_species(name: str, limit: int = 100) -> list:
    """Fetch species occurrences from GBIF.

    Args:
        name: Scientific or common name to search.
        limit: Maximum number of records to fetch.

    Returns:
        List of occurrence dictionaries.

    Raises:
        ConnectionError: If GBIF API is unreachable.
    """

docs.title: Type Hints
docs.text:
Use type hints for function signatures:

def create_layer(
    name: str,
    occurrences: list[dict],
    crs: str = "EPSG:4326"
) -> QgsVectorLayer:
    ...

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• Write tests for new functionality
  • Maintain or improve code coverage
  • Tests should be independent and repeatable
def test_fetch_species_returns_list():
    """Test that fetch_species returns a list."""
    result = fetch_species("Panthera leo")
    assert isinstance(result, list)

docs.title: Before Submitting
docs.text:
• Code passes all linting checks
  • Tests pass and coverage is maintained
  • Documentation is updated if needed
  • Commit messages are clear

docs.title: PR Description
docs.text:
Include in your PR description:

  • Summary of changes
  • Related issue numbers (fixes #123)
  • Screenshots for UI changes
  • Testing instructions

docs.title: Review Process
docs.text:
1. Maintainers will review your PR
  2. Address any requested changes
  3. Once approved, it will be merged
  4. You'll be credited in the release notes

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
Releases are managed by maintainers:

  1. Update version in metadata.txt
  2. Update CHANGELOG
  3. Create and push version tag
  4. GitHub Actions builds and publishes release

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Questions: Open a Discussion
  • Bugs: Open an Issue
  • Chat: Contact Kartoza

docs.title: Recognition
docs.text:
Contributors are recognized in:

  • Release notes
  • README credits section
  • This documentation

Thank you for contributing to Species Explorer!

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text: This guide covers setting up your development environment for Species Explorer.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Git for version control
  • QGIS 3.0+ or Nix for QGIS provision
  • Python 3.9+
  • Nix (recommended) for reproducible environment

docs.title: 1. Install Nix
docs.text:
If you don't have Nix installed:

curl -L https://nixos.org/nix/install | sh

Enable flakes in your Nix configuration:

mkdir -p ~/.config/nix
echo "experimental-features = nix-command flakes" >> ~/.config/nix/nix.conf

docs.title: 2. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer.git
cd SpeciesExplorer

docs.title: 3. Enter Development Shell
docs.text:
nix develop

This provides:

  • Python environment with all dependencies
  • QGIS
  • Code quality tools (black, flake8, isort, pylint)
  • Testing tools (pytest, coverage)
  • Documentation tools (mkdocs)
  • Pre-commit hooks

docs.title: 4. Allow direnv (Optional)
docs.text:
If you use direnv:

direnv allow

The environment will activate automatically when entering the directory.

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer.git
cd SpeciesExplorer

docs.title: 2. Create Virtual Environment
docs.text:
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate  # Linux/macOS
# or
.venv\Scripts\activate  # Windows

docs.title: 3. Install Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install -r REQUIREMENTS.txt
pip install -r REQUIREMENTS_TESTING.txt

docs.title: 4. Install QGIS
docs.text: Install QGIS 3.x from your system package manager or qgis.org.

docs.title: Plugin Symlink
docs.text:
To test the plugin in QGIS, create a symlink:

LinuxmacOSWindows 
ln -s $(pwd)/species_explorer ~/.local/share/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/default/python/plugins/SpeciesExplorer

ln -s $(pwd)/species_explorer ~/Library/Application\ Support/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/default/python/plugins/SpeciesExplorer

mklink /D "%APPDATA%\QGIS\QGIS3\profiles\default\python\plugins\SpeciesExplorer" "species_explorer"

Or use the Nix command:

nix run .#symlink

docs.title: Neovim
docs.text:
The repository includes .exrc and .nvim.lua for Neovim integration:

  • WhichKey shortcuts under <leader>p
  • LSP configuration with Pyright
  • DAP debugging support

docs.title: VS Code
docs.text:
Recommended extensions:

  • Python
  • Pylance
  • QGIS Plugin Development

Create .vscode/settings.json:

{
  "python.analysis.extraPaths": [
    "/usr/share/qgis/python",
    "/usr/share/qgis/python/plugins"
  ],
  "python.formatting.provider": "black",
  "python.linting.enabled": true,
  "python.linting.flake8Enabled": true
}

docs.title: PyCharm
docs.text:
1. Set Python interpreter to your QGIS Python
  2. Add QGIS Python paths to project structure
  3. Enable Black formatter

docs.title: Pre-commit Hooks
docs.text:
Install pre-commit hooks:

pre-commit install

Run checks manually:

pre-commit run --all-files

Or using Nix:

nix run .#checks

docs.title: Compile Resources
docs.text:
If you modify resources.qrc:

pyrcc5 -o species_explorer/resources.py species_explorer/resources.qrc

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
# With Nix
nix run .#test

# Without Nix
pytest test/ -v --cov=species_explorer

docs.title: Building Documentation
docs.text:
# Serve locally
nix run .#docs-serve

# Build static site
nix run .#docs-build

docs.title: QGIS Python Not Found
docs.text:
Add QGIS to your Python path:

export PYTHONPATH="/usr/share/qgis/python:$PYTHONPATH"

docs.title: Pre-commit Fails
docs.text:
• Ensure all tools are installed
  • Run pre-commit clean and try again
  • Check specific hook errors

docs.title: Tests Fail
docs.text:
• Ensure QGIS is properly configured
  • Check that all dependencies are installed
  • Run with -v for verbose output

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: User Guide
docs.text: Welcome to the Species Explorer User Guide! This guide will help you get started with exploring biodiversity data in QGIS.

docs.title: What is Species Explorer?
docs.text: Species Explorer is a QGIS plugin that connects to the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) to fetch and visualize species occurrence data. GBIF is the world's largest biodiversity database, containing over 2 billion occurrence records from around the globe.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
• Installation

    Learn how to install Species Explorer in QGIS

  • Quick Start

    Get up and running in 5 minutes

  • Usage Guide

    Detailed guide on all features

  • FAQ

    Frequently asked questions

docs.title: Species Occurrence Data
docs.text:
An occurrence record is a documented observation of a species at a specific location and time. This can include:

  • Museum specimen collections
  • Field observations
  • Citizen science records
  • Research survey data

docs.title: GBIF Integration
docs.text:
Species Explorer uses the GBIF REST API to:

  1. Search for species by name
  2. Retrieve taxonomic information
  3. Download occurrence records
  4. Create point layers in QGIS

docs.title: Data Attributes
docs.text:
Each occurrence point includes attributes such as:

Attribute Description gbifID Unique GBIF identifier scientificName Full scientific name decimalLatitude Latitude coordinate decimalLongitude Longitude coordinate eventDate Date of observation basisOfRecord Type of record (specimen, observation, etc.) countryCode ISO country code

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
• Check the FAQ for common questions
  • Report issues on GitHub
  • Contact Kartoza for commercial support

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: What is GBIF?
docs.text: The Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) is an international network and data infrastructure that provides open access to biodiversity data. It hosts over 2 billion occurrence records from thousands of institutions worldwide.

docs.title: Is Species Explorer free?
docs.text: Yes! Species Explorer is free and open-source software, licensed under the GPL-2.0 license. GBIF data is also freely accessible.

docs.title: What QGIS versions are supported?
docs.text: Species Explorer supports QGIS 3.0 and later. We recommend using the latest stable or LTR version for best compatibility.

docs.title: How current is the GBIF data?
docs.text: GBIF data is continuously updated as data providers publish new records. When you fetch data, you're getting the latest available records from GBIF.

docs.title: Can I use this data commercially?
docs.text: GBIF data is open data, but individual datasets may have specific licenses. Always check the data citation requirements and respect the licenses of individual publishers.

docs.title: Why are some coordinates missing?
docs.text:
Some GBIF records don't include coordinates for various reasons:

  • Historical records without GPS
  • Sensitive species locations obscured
  • Data quality issues

Species Explorer only imports records with valid coordinates.

docs.title: What's the data accuracy?
docs.text:
Data accuracy varies by source:

  • Museum specimens: Generally high accuracy
  • Citizen science: Variable accuracy
  • Historical records: May have lower precision

Check the coordinateUncertaintyInMeters field for precision information.

docs.title: Why is my download slow?
docs.text:
Large datasets can take time to download. Factors include:

  • Number of records (millions for common species)
  • Internet connection speed
  • GBIF server load

docs.title: Can I limit the number of records?
docs.text: Currently, Species Explorer fetches all available records. You can filter results after import using QGIS tools.

docs.title: Does it work offline?
docs.text: No, Species Explorer requires an internet connection to fetch data from GBIF. However, once downloaded, the data is stored locally in your QGIS project.

docs.title: How do I report a bug?
docs.text:
Report bugs on our GitHub Issues page. Please include:

  • QGIS version
  • Plugin version
  • Steps to reproduce
  • Error messages

docs.title: Plugin won't load
docs.text:
1. Check QGIS version (3.0+ required)
  2. Verify plugin is enabled in Plugin Manager
  3. Check for Python errors in View → Log Messages

docs.title: No results for my species
docs.text:
• Try the scientific name
  • Check spelling
  • Some species have limited GBIF records

docs.title: Data not displaying correctly
docs.text:
• Check CRS settings
  • Ensure coordinates are valid (lat/lon)
  • Try zooming to layer extent

Still have questions? Open an issue or contact us at info@kartoza.com.

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: This guide covers how to install Species Explorer in QGIS.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• QGIS 3.0 or later
  • Internet connection (for fetching data from GBIF)

docs.title: Method 1: QGIS Plugin Manager (Recommended)
docs.text:
1. Open QGIS
  2. Go to Plugins → Manage and Install Plugins
  3. Search for "Species Explorer"
  4. Click Install Plugin

docs.title: Method 2: Install from ZIP
docs.text:
1. Download the latest release from GitHub Releases
  2. Open QGIS
  3. Go to Plugins → Manage and Install Plugins
  4. Click Install from ZIP
  5. Select the downloaded ZIP file
  6. Click Install Plugin

docs.title: Method 3: Manual Installation
docs.text:
1. Download or clone the repository: 

    git clone https://github.com/kartoza/SpeciesExplorer.git

  2. Copy the species_explorer folder to your QGIS plugins directory:

    LinuxWindowsmacOS 
    cp -r species_explorer ~/.local/share/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/default/python/plugins/

    Copy-Item -Recurse species_explorer "$env:APPDATA\QGIS\QGIS3\profiles\default\python\plugins\"

    cp -r species_explorer ~/Library/Application\ Support/QGIS/QGIS3/profiles/default/python/plugins/

  3. Restart QGIS

  4. Enable the plugin in Plugins → Manage and Install Plugins

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
After installation:

  1. Check that Species Explorer appears in the Plugins menu
  2. Look for the Species Explorer icon in the toolbar
  3. Click the icon to open the plugin dialog

docs.title: Plugin Not Showing
docs.text:
• Make sure the plugin is enabled in Plugin Manager
  • Try restarting QGIS
  • Check the QGIS log for errors (View → Log Messages)

docs.title: Network Issues
docs.text:
• Ensure you have an active internet connection
  • Check if GBIF (gbif.org) is accessible
  • Corporate firewalls may block the connection

docs.title: Python Errors
docs.text:
• Ensure your QGIS installation has the required Python packages
  • Check the Python console for detailed error messages

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Once installed, proceed to the Quick Start guide to begin using Species Explorer.

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get started with Species Explorer in just a few minutes!

docs.title: Step 1: Open Species Explorer
docs.text: Click the Species Explorer icon in the toolbar, or go to Plugins → Species Explorer → Species Explorer.

docs.title: Step 2: Search for a Species
docs.text:
1. Enter a species name in the search box (e.g., "Panthera leo" or "lion")
  2. Click the Find button
  3. A list of matching species will appear

Search Tips

  • Use scientific names for more accurate results
  • Partial names work too (e.g., "Panthera")
  • Common names are supported for many species

docs.title: Step 3: Select a Species
docs.text:
1. Click on a species in the results list
  2. The taxonomy information will be displayed
  3. Review the species details to confirm your selection

docs.title: Step 4: Fetch Occurrence Data
docs.text:
1. Click the Fetch button
  2. Wait for the data to download from GBIF
  3. A new point layer will be added to your project

Download Time

Large datasets may take longer to download. The status bar shows progress.

docs.title: Step 5: Explore the Data
docs.text:
Your new layer includes:

  • Point locations for each occurrence record
  • Attribute table with detailed information
  • Default styling with clustered visualization

docs.title: Example: African Lion
docs.text:
Let's explore the distribution of the African Lion (Panthera leo):

  1. Search for "Panthera leo"
  2. Select the species from results
  3. Click Fetch
  4. View the occurrence points across Africa

The result shows museum specimens, field observations, and citizen science records from across the species' range.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Learn about all features in the Usage Guide
  • Customize the visualization with QGIS styling tools
  • Export data for further analysis
  • Combine with other GIS layers for ecological analysis

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub

docs.title: Usage Guide
docs.text: This guide covers all features and functionality of Species Explorer.

docs.title: Interface Overview
docs.text:
The Species Explorer dialog has three main sections:

  1. Search Area - Enter species names and search
  2. Results List - Browse matching species
  3. Action Buttons - Fetch data or clear results

docs.title: Scientific Names
docs.text:
Enter the full or partial scientific name:

  • Full name: Panthera leo
  • Genus only: Panthera
  • Partial: leo

docs.title: Common Names
docs.text:
Many species can be found by common name:

  • lion
  • african elephant
  • blue whale

docs.title: Search Tips
docs.text:
Best Practices

  • Scientific names provide more accurate results
  • GBIF may have multiple entries for synonyms
  • Check the taxonomy to verify you have the correct species

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
When you click Fetch:

  1. Species Explorer queries the GBIF API
  2. Occurrence records are downloaded
  3. A point layer is created in QGIS
  4. Default styling is applied

docs.title: Data Limits
docs.text:
GBIF may return thousands of records. Consider:

  • Large datasets may take time to download
  • Memory usage increases with record count
  • You can filter data later in QGIS

docs.title: Layer Structure
docs.text:
Each occurrence layer includes:

Field Type Description gbifID Integer Unique identifier scientificName String Full scientific name decimalLatitude Float Latitude decimalLongitude Float Longitude eventDate String Observation date basisOfRecord String Record type countryCode String ISO country code institutionCode String Data provider

docs.title: Styling
docs.text:
The default style uses:

  • Point markers with species-appropriate colors
  • Clustering for dense areas
  • Labels on hover

You can customize styling using standard QGIS tools.

docs.title: Filtering
docs.text:
Filter your data using:

  • Attribute Table filters
  • Select by Expression
  • Definition Queries

Example expression to filter recent records: 

"eventDate" > '2020-01-01'

docs.title: Export Options
docs.text:
Export occurrence data to:

  • Shapefile - For GIS compatibility
  • GeoPackage - Modern, recommended format
  • CSV - For spreadsheet analysis
  • GeoJSON - For web applications

docs.title: Combining with Other Data
docs.text:
Species occurrence data works well with:

  • Protected Areas - Assess species within reserves
  • Climate Data - Model species distributions
  • Land Cover - Analyze habitat preferences
  • Elevation - Understand altitudinal ranges

docs.title: Spatial Analysis
docs.text:
Use QGIS tools for:

  • Heatmaps - Visualize occurrence density
  • Kernel Density - Estimate species density
  • Point Pattern Analysis - Analyze distribution patterns
  • Species Richness - Count species per area

docs.title: No Results Found
docs.text:
• Check spelling of species name
  • Try alternative names or synonyms
  • Use scientific name instead of common name

docs.title: Missing Coordinates
docs.text: Some GBIF records lack coordinates. These are filtered out during import.

docs.title: Slow Downloads
docs.text:
• Large datasets take time
  • Check internet connection
  • GBIF API may be under high load

Made with 💗 by Kartoza | Donate | GitHub
